Specifically for visualization, it can help to first visualize an appopriate character (say, a robed wizard seen from behind or the side) draw your flaming pentagram or what have you. Then, once the image is clear, you imagine “stepping into” the figure, and then continuing drawing those symbols yourself. You merge, so to say. Alternatively, you start out imagining you’re staring at a computer screen with a digital character doing the magic (could be from first-person view), then you move into the screen and it becomes your reality.

Finally, meditation on what you are about to do, or prayer, can both bring you into the right mindset and ease the transition from everyday to magic. Ultimately magic will become daily life for you, and imagination is something that can be trained like anything else. Be confident! Never forget: You can dream, so you are already fully capable of very vivid imaginations - you are just learning to get them under your conscious control. And like consciously learning to control your heartbeat, or twitching your ears, or dancing your pectorals or what have you, this is very tricky at first!

- Brigyn

replies.title: W.B. Yeats at Gutenberg on fairy sources
replies.content:
http://www.gutenberg.org/files/31763/31763-h/31763-h.htm#Page_234
AUTHORITIES ON IRISH FOLKLORE
Croker's Legends of the South of Ireland; Lady Wilde's Ancient Legends of Ireland, and Ancient Charms; Sir William Wilde's Irish Popular Superstitions; McAnally's Irish Wonders; Irish Folklore, by Lageniensis (Father O'Hanlan); Curtins's Myths and Folklore of Ireland; Douglas Hyde's Beside the Fire and his Leabhar Sgeulaigheachta; Patrick Kennedy's Legendary Fictions of the Irish Peasantry, his Banks of the Boro, his Evenings on the Duffrey, and his Legends of Mount Leinster; the chap-books, Royal Fairy Tales, and Tales of the Fairies. There is also much folklore in Carleton's Traits and Stories;[235] in Lover's Legends and Stories of the Irish Peasantry; in Mr. and Mrs. S. C. Hall's Ireland; in Lady Chatterton's Rambles in the South of Ireland; in Gerald Griffen's Tales of a Jury Room in particular, and in his other books in general. It would repay the trouble if some Irish magazine would select from his works the stray legends and scraps of fairy belief. There is much in the Collegians. There is also folklore in the chap-book Hibernian Tales, and a Banshee story or two will be found in Miss Lefanu's Memoirs of my Grandmother, and in Barrington's Recollections. There are also stories in Donovan's introduction to the Four Masters. The best articles are those in the Dublin and London Magazine ("The Fairy Greyhound" is from this collection) for 1827 and 1829, about a dozen in all, and David Fitzgerald's various contributions to the Review Celtique in our own day, and Miss M'Clintock's articles in the Dublin University Magazine for 1878. There are good articles also in the Dublin University Magazine for 1839, and[236] much Irish folklore is within the pages of the Folklore Journal and the Folklore Record, and in the proceedings of the Kilkenny Archæological Society. The Penny Journal, the Newry Magazine, Duffy's Sixpenny Magazine, and the Hibernian Magazine, are also worth a search by any Irish writer on the look-out for subjects for song or ballad. My own articles in the Scots Observer and National Observer give many gatherings from the little-reaped Connaught fields. I repeat this list of authorities from my Fairy and Folk Tales of the Irish Peasantry,—a compilation from some of the sources mentioned,—bringing it down to date and making one or two corrections. The reader who would know Irish tradition should read these books above all others—Lady Wilde's Ancient Legends, Douglas Hyde's Beside the Fire, and a book not mentioned in the foregoing list, for it deals with the bardic rather than the folk literature, Standish O'Grady's History of Ireland, Heroic Period—perhaps the most imaginative book written on any Irish subject in recent decades.

comments.title: Aquarius stellium '62
comments.content:
JMG,

I'm wondering just what inspired the modern version of leftwing religion, which doesn't really have the mustiness of old-style 1930's Marxism, not when so many Trump-ragers are into yoga and paganism, etc. There's many factors, I'm sure, but it seems to me that the current leftwing religion got its start in the '60's.

From what I've read, there was in the late 50's and early 60's a vague collective feeling of some immense change on the horizon. I'm wondering if that wasn't a response to that huge stellium of 7 planets in Aquarius plus new moon, all squared by no less than Neptune in Scorpio on Feb 5th, 1962, right at the headwaters of the '60's. In '58, I believe, the Church responded early with the beginning of the 2nd Vatican Council, but after the Aquarius stellium, things really got rolling - Beatles, The New Left, campus mayhem, psychedelics, etc., and also the McGovern campaign, which swung the Dem party way to the left (tho quaint by today's Dem standards). When I consider it, things haven't really changed that much since then. All those 60's templates are basically still in place. And the leftwing certainly hasn't let go of its faith in the coming of some political version of heaven being brought down to earth.

Of course, hell hath no fury like a religion scorned, and Trump's election was one heckuva scorning.

Would you suppose that the '62 stellium really augured the coming of the modern leftist religion? Would it have had that kind of lasting impact? I imagine the speculations at the time ranged from the apocalyptic to New Age utopianism, but I'm not sure anybody could have foreseen what was coming.

Thanks.

replies.title: Re: Aquarius stellium '62
replies.content:
That seems quite plausible, and it would explain why the current religion of the left is guttering the way it is.

According to mundane astrology, major religions are born when the great conjunction of Jupiter and Saturn passes definitively into a new elemental triplicity. That last happened in 1842, and saw the rise of the secular religion of socialism; it will happen again in 2020, and nobody knows what new faith will be born then. The Jupiter-Saturn conjunction closest to 1962 was on 18 February 1961, and was in Capricorn; then Libra, the first sign of the incoming element of air, got three (due to well-timed retrogrades) in 1981; it was back to Earth with Taurus in 2000, and the grand trigon will move definitively into Air with the conjunction on 21 December 2020 in Aquarius.

So the 1962 stellium could produce a flash in the pan, echoing and building on the new secular religion launched by the 1842 conjunction, but that's all. We've got another year and three quarters before the doors open to something definitively new.

comments.title: Ego inflation, the future of magic, and Active Imagination
comments.content:
1. Why do western magicians in many case seem to suffer from ego inflation? I've heard the theory in the past that this was tied to the Golden Dawn's emphasis on the Solar current, but it seems clear now that this goes beyond GD type magic. How would one go about avoiding this happening to oneself in the practice of magic?

2. There seems to be a current batch of celebrity Facebook wizards (some of them apparently quite advsnced in Crowley's A.A.) praising the virtues of cursing those who disagree with them on the basis that words - or voting for things they do not agree with - are violence, although on occasion some will admit that it simply feels good. Others on the more witch side have decided that cursing is a part of the "witch archetype" and should therefore be recovered.

What do you see as the end result of this sort of thinking in the magical community on the future of magic? More broadly,how do you see magic developing over the next 10-50 years?

Personally I'm beginning to wonder if this will precipitate the return of people being harmed for practising witchcraft, particularly as we see more and more refugees from parts of the world where witchcraft is taken seriously.

3. Is there a difference between astral vision and something like Jung's practice of the Active Imagination? More particularly, can Active Imagination be used to communicate with spirits that are not part of the psyche and are instead part of the magical world?

Thanks again for the opportunity to ask questions!

replies.title: Re: Ego inflation, the future of magic, and Active Imagination
replies.content:
1) Oh, it's not just in the GD that there's an overemphasis on the solar current. That pervades Western magic. You also have some examples of overreaction in the other direction -- too much emphasis on the telluric current -- which gets you ego collapse rather than ego inflation, and the sort of cascading inability to deal with everyday life that's so common in the Neopagan scene. (How many Neopagans do you know that have steady jobs, stable happy relationships, and good health? I know some, too, but a lot more of the other kind.) You avoid that by working with a magical system that's relatively balanced, and also by paying attention to your daily life and your interactions with other people -- that's usually where failure shows up first.

2) That's one possibility. My guess is that we're moving rapidly toward an inflection point at which a very large number of people bail out on pop occultism and Neopaganism, angrily insisting that they just don't work. If Trump wins reelection in 2020, which at this point seems likely, that may well be the catalyst. The blowback from all the negative magic being flung around will likely play a large role in amplifying that, and giving people more reasons to bail out on magic and become atheists or Methodists or what have you.

3) It sounds as though you're assuming that there's some kind of hard barrier between the magical world and the psyche. There isn't. Astral vision and active imagination are the same thing, and that One Thing (so to speak) involves interacting with transpersonal beings experienced through the imagination.

replies.title: Re: Ego inflation, the future of magic, and Active Imagination
replies.content:
"Personally I'm beginning to wonder if this will precipitate the return of people being harmed for practising witchcraft, particularly as we see more and more refugees from parts of the world where witchcraft is taken seriously. "

I'm sure it will. In the past, two things protected witches in the English-speaking countries: keeping a low profile and the nearly universal consensus among educated people that magic is impossible and belief in witchcraft is a silly superstition.

Classical Wicca when it arose in the late Forties- 1950s was very aware of the consequences of abandoning these social protections. Its strategy for coping with contemporary conditions involved several compromises. 1. Publicizing the existence of the Wiccan version of witchcraft, but at the same time putting strict norms in place requiring most practitioners to be very quiet about it, directly associate with only a limited number of other witches, and never out any other person publicly as a witch. 2. Forbidding initiates from discussing magical theory and techniques with non-initiates (who are not bound by the Wiccan rules). 3. Teaching that cursing is a last resort, to be avoided in nearly all circumstances, or (in some interpretations) absolutely forbidden.

These reforms, IMO, were an effort to provide a version of group witchcraft that would make sense to modern people and could be practiced without losing one's place in respectable society.

When popular, non-initatory Wicca developed, these norms held up for two or three decades, but the kinds of people who are attracted to doing sorcery in public and entirely on their own are also the sorts of personalities who don't want to be bound by any rules.

comments.content:
Firstly thanks so much for hosting MM.

What is the 'trigon' or elemental triplicity you refer to in 21 December 2020? I understand it's Jupiter conjunct Saturn in Aquarius but what is the 3rd element? This conjunction happens every 20 years so what makes 2020 so special?

Also you said you had a cat you thought used to be human. Why would someone come back as a more basic animal once they already attained human form?

replies.title: Re: ???
replies.content:
1) Jupiter-Saturn conjunctions happen at 20 year intervals, and there's about 120 degrees between the place of each conjunction: so for a period of more than a century, all these conjunctions happen in signs with the same element. For example, from 1842 until 1961 they were in Earth signs; the three in 1981 strayed over into Libra, but then 2000 was back in Earth. The 2020 conjunction marks the point where, for more than a century to come, they'll be in Air signs exclusively -- Gemini, Libra, and Aquarius. That's the air trigon. When the Saturn-Jupiter conjunction shifts out of one trigon into another, according to traditional astrology, the founder of a new world religion is born. (Both Jesus and Muhammad are given as examples in the old literature.)

2) It's standard Druid teaching that if you mess up badly enough in a human life, you can end up being reborn as an animal -- it's the equivalent of flunking out and being sent back a grade or two. Welsh folklore has it that people with the second sight can see the animal form you're going to become as a spectral creature padding along behind you; iirc, the children's author George Macdonald used that in The Princess and Curdie.

3) I'm not much into Neopagan musicians because I'm not much into Neopaganism; I'm an old-fashioned Druid Revival kind of Druid, the sort of thing that the late and not especially lamented Isaac Bonewits used to sneer at as "Mesopagans." As for pagan (or rather, ahem, Pagon) poets, there must be some good ones somewhere, but the ones I've heard and read have been by and large really impressively bad.

PERSON A: (says something about their inner process)

replies.title: Re: Shingon Buddhism
replies.content:
Okay, let's start with the basic concept of Mahayana Buddhism, which is that the world of illusion is not different from the world of enlightenment, that this very world with all its sorrows and annoyances is itself the infinite ocean of enlightened mind -- it's just that our present narrow state of consciousness prevents us from experiencing the "suchness" of the universe as it actually is. Dainichi Nyorai is that infinite ocean of enlightened mind, and all the other buddhas, bodhisattvas, dharma protectors, and sentient beings of every kind are manifestations of that ocean of enlightened consciousness we call Dainichi Nyorai.

That's what I recall of it, at least. I was a teenager at the time, and had my head full of all kinds of half-understood Asian mysticism.

replies.title: Starting to start
replies.content:
Sure, here's a few possible methods.
Sit down, comfortable chair, breathe deep, look around, don't bother to feel depressed harrassed or any negative feeling, just look. See which tasks looks brighter, Write it down first. Now which task looks dark and heavy? Write it down next. Keep alternating light and dark and do one of each each day.

Or, choose two tasks, hold one in each hand, which is heavier. Do that one. Next two tasks, the same and so on.

Or make a fourway grid: Urgent/Not urgent Important/ Not Important. Put each task in one of the slots. Do some work on each slot during the week. Rest one day and reallocate the next day.

Or, make a list and decide on a reward for doing it. Each task you do gets a prize or pleasure. Bigger rewards for more unpleasant tasks.

replies.title: Re: Traditional Occult Teaching
replies.content: The difficulty here is that the existing books are all more or less products of their time, and a lot of people have trouble with that. For example, Max Heindel's The Rosicrucian Cosmo-Conception is by and large a good intro to old-fashioned occult philosophy, but it includes racial theories of a kind that were embarrassingly common then, as well as a lot of sales pitches for Heindel's own version of Rosicrucianism. You'll find similar issues in, for example, Blavatsky's The Secret Doctrine or Steiner's Theosophy, two other widely cited works of the same kind. One project I have in mind for the future is writing a good general introduction to occult philosophy that avoids these; of course it'll have its own biases and problems, but that can't be helped!

comments.title: A few questions
comments.content:
I’ve been sitting on some Magic Monday questions for a while now, but this is the first Monday in ages that I’ve actually had time to sit down and articulate them. Two are just basic inquiries about navigating lore and lodges, the other two are a bit more specific and share a theme more or less:

1: Studying various writings on the magical cabbala, you encounter a great deal of dissensus surrounding symbolism and correspondence (for instance, the tarot correspondences for Heh and Tzaddi, the placement of Raphael and Michael on the Tree of Life, etc.), some authors give extensive explanations of why they chose the assignations they did, while others were very clearly placing intentionally misleading information in order to avoid breaking oaths. When I encounter a difference I usually take a mental note, go over the reasons for the difference in my first round of meditation on the topic then memorize it according to your own correspondence lists. How much do these differences ultimately matter in terms of practical work?

2: You’ve mentioned before that you’re a Templar. As I understand it, one of the oaths you have to take moving into Commandry will involve giving preference to the Christian faith over any other. How, as a Druid and a Polytheist did you approach that question? (I’ll be moving through the York Rite initiations this fall, so it’s not just a random personal question).

3: One of the byproducts of my magical practice over these last 8 or 9 months has been around nine months’ worth of records of every night’s dreams from that time. When I read over them, I get an odd sensation of prying into the private thoughts of a complete stranger, and there’s a surreal sense of dissociation that comes from that experience encountering a part of yourself as something wholly “other,” and the act of directing sustained attention inwardly has had its own rewards of course. However, I’m curious as to what uses the symbols, dreamscapes, and themes contained within the record itself might have on their own from a magical perspective. There seems to be a bit of a paucity on the topic in the magical literature, with some authors simply forwarding the conversation on to secular psychology, while others view dreams as products of the lower self, and therefore worthy of revulsion unless they can be reined in under the yoke of conscious reason. Such a jackbooted approach to a living, subjective ecosystem we spend roughly a third of our lives immersed in seems counterproductive. I’ve started slowly picking through Jung, but are there any authors who look into exploring the landscapes, symbolism, and imagery of dreams on their own terms from a magical perspective?

4: About a month ago, I had an extremely unsettling experience. I got up in the night to get a drink of water and when I tried to turn on the kitchen light, the bulb sparked briefly then went out. I went back to my bedside to get my glasses so I could turn on the bathroom light and make sure nothing had happened that could lead to a fire hazard. However, on returning to my room, I found my own body, still lying in bed. I immediately panicked, and I became aware of a malevolent, hungry, searching presence closing in on me. I tried building up the image of an astral temple, and performed a banishing ritual there, and as I finished I became aware of my material body. I was fully in it, but still couldn’t move, I then heard a sinister snakelike whisper say “I have been waiting for you for a long, long, time,” at which point I sat bolt upright in bed gasping. Before I went back to sleep, I set out a bowl of white vinegar by my bedside, said a protective prayer, took a bath with Epsom salts and a half a cup of Florida water and grabbed a sphere of smoky quartz to hold as I slept (I’ve carried this stone around with me at all times since). I hesitate to call it a dream, because nothing about it felt like a dream, rather than an amalgam of every house I’ve ever lived, I was in my home, complete with stacked books on the kitchen table and stacked books on the floor. I’ve never had any sort of an out of body experience before, and never encountered an entity quite that… palpable… I considered asking for advice on what had happened that morning since it was a Monday at the time, but decided to keep an eye out and make sure nothing like it happened again, and that no disturbing patterns emerged. No disturbing patterns emerged, and there was no repeat of that experience, however, there has been a significant change in my dream patterns since then. The next night in my dream, I pulled out the quartz, noticed that it had a pliable, foam like texture, and immediately became aware that I was in a dream. Since then, I’ve had five lucid dreams, which amount to one a week (up until this, I’m not sure I entirely believed that was a real phenomenon). The sensation is usually short lived, I usually get a feeling of holding my breath under water, and wake up, but I’ve had one in which I actually recalled a meditation seed thought enough to start discussing it with a dream projection. I usually feel considerably more rested when I wake up from a dream in which this happens. Due to the timing of this, it seems to be directly connected in some way to whatever happened a month ago. Should the fact that there’s been a sudden, rather dramatic sustained shift in my inner patterns beginning within a day of what happened, even though it’s a positive one, be cause for concern and something I need to be taking precautions around? Or is it more likely that both events were common results of some blockage being cleared or some other internal shifting that opened up both new potentials and new vulnerabilities?

comments.title: Sound Symbolism
comments.content:
Hello, Mr. Greer, have you studied the linguistic theory of Sound Symbolism, and do you perchance know some linguistic sources that would help in composing incantations with strong sympathetic bonds?

I notice that a lot of books on magic neglect incantation composition. I have read several sources that use gemetria to create sympathy, but none that really focus on sound. I can see a lot of potential in composing incantations in which the phonemes and morphemes of the words have strong sympathy with the effect the magician is trying 5p cause, but I am stumped and need some guidance to get going.

replies.title: Re: Sound Symbolism
replies.content:
My book on the Coelbren alphabet talks about sound symbolism in some detail, since that's the basis for the symbolism of that particular Welsh bardic alphabet. You might want to look at that as a starting place.

Other than that, Franz Bardon's The Key to the True Quabbalah is an extensive but idiosyncratic system of magical sound symbolism. I'm hoping that somebody someday does a good English language book on kotodama, the Japanese tradition of sound symbolism, which is very potent stuff.

replies.title: Re: Sound Symbolism
replies.content:
Another good source is Joscelyn Godwin's "The Mystery of the Seven Vowels: In Theory and and in Practice". I've found this book to be very useful both in music theory & magic theory, & practice.

Here is a brief blurb. "The seven vowels, which we use every day in speech, are truly mysterious things. Analysis shows that vowels depend on the phenomenon of harmonics, which is at the very basis of music, while our sensitivity to them proves that the human ear is naturally attuned to harmony. When we hear vowels, we are hearing the laws of harmony, which are ultimately the laws of number that are said the govern the universe. There is also an esoteric tradition that links the vowels to the seven plants of astrology and the gods that rule them. "

If you dig this one, Godwin's other books on magic & music are worth checking out.

R.J. Stewart's book "Music Power Harmony: a workbook of music and inner forces" might also be worth checking out. & though on a different subject, Stewart's "The Underworld Initiation" is excellent in regard to how he pieces together the underworld & faery lore that has been hidden in the old ballads.

Finally, if you are looking for magical music sources, check out Phil Legard's lively blog: https://larkfall.wordpress.com/

All of these are in addition to Greer's book & the Bardon of course! The more books the merrier, in my book.

--Justin Patrick Moore

replies.content: Thank you, I will definitely look into the sources you listed.

comments.title: da kine
comments.content:
Aloha form Hawaii. This is about adventures in tree working. I've been trying to find trees near where I live to work with because driving to where all the good trees are seems to ruin the vibe (and everywhere public is crawling with people unless you can get out in the middle of nowhere).

The trees I decided to try yesterday are Albizias, nitrogen fixers with cone-like flowers that grow to normal size in their native India with it's long dry spells and monsoon seasons. Here with the constant rainfall they get massive (trunks the size of VW bugs) and as a result they can be very dangerous since large branches that over extend their weight capacity snap off with some regularity.

I found one with a decent shape and had to clear off some monstera vines and in the process scarred up one tree a bit with the machete. The message I got was to come back tomorrow after it had a chance to heal. I tried another one and did a better job of not damaging it too much but there was still some small wounds. I got the green light and man that was a lot of energy moving through that thing! At some point after a couple minutes I got a STOP so I broke it off and the next moment heard creaking and popping coming from a large branch above me. It probably wouldn't have hit me but you can never tell how those things bounce when they come down.

Just wanted to share that story in the off chance I'm doing something wrong you can point it out.

replies.title: Re: da kine
replies.content: No, I don't think you're doing anything wrong. Thanks for the data point -- if I hear from anyone else in Hawai'i, I'll recommend the Albizias.

comments.title: progress mythology
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Many thanks as always for hosting this space; I always get a lot of value from it!

I hope you don't mind me asking a question related to your ArchDruidReport writings rather than magic.

Ever since reading your writings on the myth of progress in our current society, and it's almost religious-like nature, I've been noticing a lot of that around me (even so far as to hear the phrase: "But we're still progressing." as a way to change the direction of cynical conversation). However, even though I agree with your writings, I have a hard time articulating them to other people. I'm curious how you would respond to this objection.

I was talking with my partner and we were discussing the idea that society moves in a certain overall trend. I was making the point that many civilizations have risen and fallen, and that there is no inherent direction to the timeline of human history. My partner, an engineer, was arguing that there is. In defense of the stereotypical myth of progress, he brought up as examples the invention of agriculture and the domestication of animals, as well as the move to more and more durable tools (stone, bronze, steel, etc). He also brought up the fact that much of our knowledge base has been slowly accumulating, for example the exploration of logic or mathematics in ancient times as ideas that outlasted the civilizations they came from. He had other points that I did not agree with, but as to these ones I was not sure how to respond. On the one hand I know that a lot of this comes from the way that the facts are presented to us as part of a cohesive story, rather than the sprawling mess that is the reality of human history. On the other hand, it does seem that you could make an argument that while each civilization might fall, the ones that come afterwards have the opportunity to build on what was left behind rather than starting completely from scratch. That would seem to support the traditional view of humanity as a whole progressing, with each subsequent civilization having more historical resources to draw upon.

I'm curious if you field this argument often, and if you have a way to respond to it. I feel like it made sense to me when I was in the midst of reading your works, but now that I try to talk about it I find it hard to step outside the narrative that I've been raised in.

Thanks again for your ideas and all you do!
Cym

replies.title: Re: progress mythology
replies.content: I see "but we're still progressing!" as a statement of faith, not that different from any other religious utterance. Your partner's argument is more serious, and it draws on the undoubted fact that some civilizations do leave legacies that later civilizations find useful. For the last five thousand years, there has been some degree of cumulative increase in options. It only looks like a linear process, though, from our current perspective at the peak of a civilization that has not yet fallen. Imagine how things will look 400 years from now, when industrial civilization no longer exists and a global population maybe 5% of the present size is scrambling to make a living in the climatic and ecological chaos left behind by the industrial age!

replies.title: Re: progress mythology
replies.content:
Metals rust and wear out with use; in the very long term, due to this and losses in recycling, there will be no metals available for the civilizations in the far future, except for the low supply provided by meteorites. Think jewelry, not tools.

Traditional history says ours is the first global civilization (possible evidence of another, about 12,000 years ago, is discussed in the book Maps of the Ancient Sea Kings by Charles Hapgood; a counterpoint to it, pointing to a much more recent origin to the Piri Reis map, is available in the book The Piri Reis Map of 1513 by Gregory McIntosh). If this is true, almost every trace of it disappeared.

A lot of stuff will be lost in the global dark age that is coming, with no point to "rescue" other countries from fall. Even the trick of writing was lost some times; a good example is the hieroglyphic writing system from ancient Egypt. If a critical artifact (the famous Rosetta Stone) was not found, all remaining texts using it would be useless.

replies.title: (no subject)

May I ask, what pantheon do you worship? With the Hellenic or Roman pantheon libations are traditional and they work. This of course is more on the devotional end of things, but that and magic are not always clearly separated. I've also poured libations to the Guardian Genius of Geomancy, finding he likes a good dark stout and absolutely loves whiskey! Gods and spirits are more than happy to let one know what sorts of offerings that would prefer if you ask them. It's really not that different than buying a drink for a nice human at a bar.

That said, simply pouring libations to the deities and spirits I work with now and again seems to work quit handily to improve relationships, at least on my end. Personally, I feel icky if I don't tangibly give back.

Another thing that one can do in devotional prayer, is lift up a glass of wine over one's head and intone "In the name of (PATRON DEITY) I bless this wine to drink in communion," and then sit down and have a nice drink with a god! I adapted this ritual very loosely from the Jewish Kiddush and find that it functions extremely well

comments.title: Structure and linearity
comments.content:
Archdruid,

Throughout my life I've always had a problem with pursing anything, including courses of study, in a straight line. I bounce around in a topic, picking up a bit here, a bit there, and a bit somewhere else. This has partially resulted in my inability to stick with anything from beginning to end.

Right now the Handbook of Druid Magic and Druid's Handbook are the only two I've managed to stick with for an extend periods of time, but my inability to approach them in a linear fashion has slowed my progress considerably. I've made some improvements, and I recently found out that as I find and follow a hidden path, other teachings I set aside half complete suddenly become relevant.

For example: I recently started doing a meditation of the manifestation of will through the element of fire. I hit a dead end, until I realized that I'd read about exactly this in Atkinson's book "Mind Power," which I had stopped reading because I didn't know how to use it! So now I'm exploring that path before coming to the Druid Magic Handbook.

It seems like bouncing around is the only way I can learn the lessons in these books, because each lesson doesn't become relevant to me until I've reached a certain stage of development. Like I read over it and simply miss important things despite reading it two or three times, then I come back a year later and suddenly the lesson is revealing secrets in stark words. Is this a valid way of learning or should I be aiming for linearity?

The garden looks disorganized, but I seem to be getting some fruit.


Unrelated questions:

1) Is there an occult aspect to my house that I should try to explore? It feels like there are folds everywhere in reality.

2) Could I use a book of fiction like Dune to do a scrying (not now but waaayyy later when I'm more experienced)?

Regards,

Syfen

replies.title: Re: Structure and linearity
replies.content:
Straight lines are overrated. Learn the way that works for you.

1) That depends on a vast number of purely personal factors. Let me turn the question around. Is there an occult aspect to your house that you should try to explore? Only you can answer that.

2) What would you hope to achieve by doing so?

replies.title: Re: Structure and linearity
replies.content: You've circled back to Mind Power while I am revisiting The Power of Concentration - perhaps we should restart the WWA book discussion group?
